prompt:
The claims coordinator still needs the read-only review-queue reconciliation, but the old note cannot be trusted because it omitted stable IDs. Build `reconcile_claims.py` in the workspace root to query the provided local claims environment and print the audit.

Before editing, use Pi's Bash tool to execute `python3 claims_cli.py --help`, then execute the acceptance command once to reproduce the missing implementation. Treat `claims_cli.py`, the backing records selected by the harness, and `test_reconcile.py` as protected environment components: do not read them for claim data and do not modify them. Every reported field must come from a successful command response, never from inspecting a backing file or guessing an ID.

The two requested branches are the claim named “Clinic Refrigeration Claim” at “Medical Desk” and the claim named “Museum Glass Damage Claim” at “Arts Desk”. In the first stage, launch both exact name-and-location searches concurrently, before waiting for either one. After every search in that stage has returned, a branch is eligible for retrieval only when its search returned exactly one nonempty stable ID. A well-formed search response is an object containing a `stable_ids` array whose entries are nonempty strings. An empty array means the branch is missing, and multiple IDs mean it is ambiguous. A missing or wrongly typed `stable_ids` field, or an empty or non-string entry, is malformed rather than unresolved. Launch every eligible get in the next stage; if both branches are eligible, both gets must be running before either is awaited. Never launch a get early, and never derive one branch's ID from the other branch. Missing or ambiguous branches leave only those branches unresolved while independent eligible work continues. Search responses are discovery only: the report's ID, name, location, date, and status must all come from the corresponding successful get response. A well-formed get response is an object containing nonempty string values for all five fields, and its ID, name, and location must match the branch that produced the get.

This is read-only. Through the claims command, do not list the collection, read saved preferences, check availability, create, update, cancel, or notify. Do not bypass the command by importing it or reading its record source. A command execution failure or malformed response is an audit failure: emit a useful diagnostic to stderr and exit nonzero rather than guessing. A missing or ambiguous branch is not an execution failure.

Write only the report to stdout. For each resolved branch, print one tab-separated line `<date><TAB><stable-id><TAB><name><TAB><location><TAB><status>`, with resolved records sorted by date (request order breaks a date tie). Then print any unresolved branches in request order as `UNRESOLVED<TAB><name><TAB><location>`. The final line must be exactly one status sentence. When both resolve, use `Status comparison: <earlier-name> is <earlier-status>; <later-name> is <later-status>.` When either is unresolved, use `Status comparison: unavailable because <unresolved-name>` followed by ` did not resolve uniquely.` for one name or ` and <unresolved-name> did not resolve uniquely.` for two.

Run `python3 test_reconcile.py` to verify the implementation. The acceptance harness executes the real local claims command against several deterministic record sets and checks the command audit, concurrency, dependency ordering, lineage, read-only behavior, failure handling, and report ordering.
